title_en: The Sixth Pillar: Belief in Divine Decree (Qadar)
title_ar: الركن السادس: الإيمان بالقدر خيره وشره
content_en:
Of the six pillars of iman, belief in divine decree (qadar) is perhaps the most philosophically profound and the most prone to misunderstanding. Al-Uthaymin devotes a full chapter to it, carefully laying out what correct belief in qadar entails and correcting two historical deviations: the Jabriyyah, who denied human choice entirely and made people like compelled automatons, and the Qadariyyah (including the Mu'tazilah), who denied Allah's comprehensive knowledge and will over human actions.

The Sheikh organizes his explanation around four levels (maratib) of belief in qadar, which together constitute the correct Athari position:

The first level is al-ilm — affirming that Allah's knowledge encompasses all things from eternity, before their creation. He knew every event that would occur, every deed every person would perform, every word ever to be spoken. This is established by numerous Quranic verses and is a matter of rational necessity, since the All-Knowing cannot be unaware of anything.

The second level is al-kitabah — affirming that Allah recorded all that would happen in al-Lawh al-Mahfuz (the Preserved Tablet) fifty thousand years before the creation of the heavens and the earth, as established in the authentic hadith of Abdullah ibn Amr reported by Muslim. This is a matter of belief in the unseen that is accepted on the basis of revelation.

The third level is al-mashee'ah — affirming that nothing happens in the universe except by Allah's will. Whatever He wills comes to be; whatever He does not will does not come to be. This includes the actions of human beings, though they act through a real will and choice that Allah created in them. Al-Uthaymin is careful here to distinguish between Allah's universal will (al-iradah al-kawniyyah), which encompasses everything including disbelief and sin, and His legislative will (al-iradah ash-shar'iyyah), which consists of what He loves and is pleased with. Allah does not love sin even though He permits it to occur in the exercise of His wisdom.

The fourth level is al-khalq — affirming that Allah is the Creator of all things, including the actions of human beings. Their movements, choices, and deeds are created by Allah, though the human being is genuinely the performer (kasib) of his own deeds. Al-Uthaymin explains this without dissolving human responsibility: the Quran holds people accountable because they act with real will and choice, and divine foreknowledge does not compel their choices.

The Sheikh addresses the common objection: 'If everything is written, why should I strive?' He answers with the hadith in which the Prophet, peace be upon him, said: 'Strive, for everyone is facilitated toward that for which he was created.' Belief in qadar is meant to produce tranquility after one has exerted effort, not passivity before it. The great companions and the righteous were firm believers in qadar and were simultaneously among the most active and determined of people.

Finally, al-Uthaymin discusses the correct response to trials and difficulties in light of qadar: patience (sabr) during affliction, gratitude (shukr) during blessings, and repentance (tawbah) after sin — never blaming qadar as an excuse for one's own wrongdoing.
